observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Management:

a. Direct the Municipal Accountant to provide reconciliation on the noted
understatement of the PPE accounts;
b. Direct the General Services Officer to conduct a complete physical
inventory of all PPE and prepare the corresponding Report on the Physical
Count of Property, Plant, and Equipment;
c. Conduct the disposal procedure for the unserviceable PPE and derecognize
the same from the books; and
d. Prepare adjusting entries to reflect the correct balance of the CIP and
disclose the CIP balance that cannot be traceable.

We recommended that Management:

a. Require the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to reconcile
entries in the Cashbook and General Ledger/Subsidiary Ledger on the
discrepancies noted and any significant findings should be resolved
accordingly; and
b. Prepare the necessary adjusting journal entries to reflect the correct
balance of the account.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Treasurer and
Municipal Accountant to investigate and record unaccounted transactions and make
necessary adjusting entries to restore accurate cash balances in the Cash in Bank -
LCCA accounts.

We also recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to
prioritize the prompt submission of the required Bank Reconciliation Statements
(BRS), along with supporting schedules to enable a timely review of the accuracy and
validity of bank balances and reconciling items.

We reiterated that Management require the:

a. GSO-designate/Inventory Committee to prepare and submit a complete
Report on the Physical Count of Inventories per type as of December 31,
2024 in order to determine the correctness of the recorded inventories in the
books of accounts of the Municipality and to ensure that semestral count of
inventory items is promptly conducted and the corresponding report is
submitted on time to the Audit Team;
b. establishment of a separate General Services Office (GSO) with stock room
for proper storage of inventories and require the coordination between the
Municipal Accountant and the GSO towards the full implementation of the
perpetual inventory method in recording of supplies and materials;
c. prepare and maintain complete and updated general and subsidiary ledgers
of accounts presented in the financial statements; and
d. come up with internal control procedures that will ensure proper handling
of inventories to prevent or minimize the risk of loss and misuse in
accordance with the provisions of the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs,
Volume I.

We recommended that the Accounting Office maintain an updated and
complete set of subsidiary ledgers and aging schedules for the unpaid obligations of the
Municipality so that the correctness and validity of related liability accounts can be
verified and established in the audit.

We also recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to
provide complete documentation and adequate disclosure for the outstanding
obligations certified or charged to Accounts Payable so that the correctness and
validity of related liability accounts can be verified and established in the audit.

We recommended that Management direct Municipal Accountant to:

a. Submit to the Audit Team copies of official receipts for remittances
pertaining to Calendar Year (CY) 2024 made in 2025;
b. Cause the thorough review of the Due to BIR account and the timely
preparation and maintenance of an appropriate Subsidiary Ledger for each
account;
c. Submit the Schedule of Due to BIR and ensure to prepare and submit this
to the Office of the Auditor; and
d. Establish a system or routine that will be observed by accounting personnel
in maintaining the subsidiary ledger and schedule of Payables to ensure that
adequate information is captured in those records.

We recommended that the Management:

a. Exert all possible efforts to reconstruct the lost or damaged financial
records using available sources such as copies from suppliers, contractors,
banks, and other third parties;
b. Establish and implement a comprehensive records management, which may
include digitizing financial documents, and ensuring that physical records
are stored in secure locations to prevent loss or damage due to natural
calamities; and
c. Direct the Municipal Accountant to strictly adhere to the submission
deadlines prescribed under COA Circular No. 2009-006, in order to avoid
suspension or disallowance in audit.

We recommended that the Management:

a. Ensure the proper granting, utilization, and liquidation of all cash advances
in accordance with COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997; and
b. Direct all Accountable Officers to immediately settle their respective Cash
Advances through demand letters.

We recommended the Management to:

a. Strictly implement the retention money provisions under Paragraphs 6.1 to
6.2 of Annex E of IRR of RA 9184; and
b. Require contractors to submit a bank guarantee, irrevocable standby letter
of credit, or surety bond callable on demand as substitute if contractors will
opt to withdraw the retention money upon full payment after completion.

We recommended the Management to assess projects that have been completed
for a year and issue a Certificate of Final Acceptance after ensuring compliance with
contract specifications.

We respectfully recommended that Management:

a. Direct the Local Engineer to prepare and submit duly Approved List of all
on-going government projects/programs/activities (PPAs) and those that
are to be implemented during the year to the Supervising Auditor and Audit
Team Leader (SA & ATL) at the beginning of the year pursuant to Item
No. 2.1 of COA Circular No. 2013-004 dated January 30, 2013 for
verification. The same shall be regularly updated during the year in case of
additional or new PPAs is to be implemented in that year, otherwise related
expenses will be disallowed in audit and legal and administrative
proceedings may be commence as evidence may warrant under the
circumstances.
b. Promptly comply with the posting requirement thru a standard Project
Signboard as designed; and
c. Submit the duly signed Monthly Monitoring Report and/or a Statement of
Work Accomplishments (SWA) based on the interval of verification (%)
percentage provided for in the Table.

We recommended that Management require the concerned offices/departments
to:

a. Implement immediately the various programs, projects and activities
funded by the NGAs, if still on-going and warranted;
b. Conduct a thorough reconciliation of the accounts and prepare the
necessary adjusting journal entries for balances requiring correction or
clarification; and and
c. Remit the unexpended balances of completed projects to the respective
source agencies or to the Bureau of the Treasury (BTr), whichever is
appropriate, the unexpended balances for the completed projects.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Budget
Officer, in coordination with the Municipal Accountant, to prepare and submit the
SCBAA for CY 2024 in compliance with the requirements of IPSAS 24. Furthermore,
Management should ensure that this statement is consistently included in all future
financial statements to promote transparency and enhance accountability.

We recommended that Management:

a. Submit to the Office of the Auditor a valid justification for our review and
evaluation why this split procurement transactions should not be disallowed
in audit for violation of the pertinent provisions of the RIRR of RA No.
9184;
b. Ensure that the procurement activities of the Municipality be properly
planned and that procurement aligns with the provisions of the Revised IRR
of RA No. 9184; and
c. Ensure the completeness of all documentary requirements before
proceeding with the payments to progress billing and final payments. Take
corrective action to address noted deficiencies and ensure adherence to legal
and regulatory requirements.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to
undertake the reconstruction of records, to the extent possible, using available
documents in order to determine the details of the unremitted balances. Depending on
the result of the examination, corrective measures, such as immediate remittance to
GSIS, Pag-IBIG, and PhilHealth or necessary adjustments in the books of accounts be
made. Furthermore, Management should institute measures to ensure the accurate and
timely remittance of statutory contributions to prevent the recurrence of unremitted
balances.

We recommended that the Management:

a. Prioritize the establishment of an environmentally compliant sanitary
landfill;
b. Ensure the establishment of BSWMCs and the setup of MRFs in each
barangay
c. Ensure thorough planning of procurement for hauling services and
maintain complete documentation for all disbursements;
d. Use the Environment / Sanitary Services (50212010) account in recognizing
the cost of garbage and hospital waste collection and disposal; and
e. Strictly comply with the environmental laws outlined in R.A. No. 9003.

We recommended that the Management:

a. Issue a certification that the activities/services cannot be provided by
regular or permanent personnel of the agency prior to the issuance of the
agreement for contract of service/job order contract;
b. Require the payees to attach a duly certified copy of the agreement/contract
by the HRMO;
c. Require each payee to prepare individual accomplishment report and Daily
Time Record duly verified and accepted by their immediate supervisor; and
d. HRMO to explain the inconsistencies in the role assignment on identified
JO workers.

We recommended that Management install adequate internal control measures
to minimize, if not stop, the recurrent errors in the preparation of checks and to charge
the responsible employees for the costs of the cancelled checks.

We recommended that Management direct:

a. Municipal Accountant to reconcile the Financial Statements and LDRRMF
Utilization Report to address the discrepancy, and ensure supporting
documents are complete and accurate; and
b. Municipal Accountant to update the Notes to Financial Statements with
current and consistent data for the reporting period, in accordance with the
Annex D format of COA Circular 2012-002.

We recommended that Management prioritize the implementation and
completion, preferably within the budget year, of all priority development
projects/programs funded out of the 20 percent EDF to attain the desired socio-
economic development and environmental outcomes of the LGU.

We also recommended that, if the originally identified projects are no longer
feasible for implementation, the concerned officials should reprogram and reclassify
the allocated budget to other eligible and implementable projects under the 20%
Development Fund (DF) that are in need of additional funding.

We recommended the Management to ensure the timely implementation of
GAD PPAs in accordance with the approved GAD Plan and Budget.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
1. Require the Municipal Accountant and the GSO-Designate to reconcile
the discrepancies between the PPE accounts recorded in the books and
the RPCPPE, and effect the necessary adjustment/s to come up with
the correct balances;
2. Instruct the GSO to maintain property cards for each PPE;
3. Direct the Inventory Committee to prepare the RPCPPE in accordance
with the required format, which reflects the costs and carrying
amounts, property numbers and other relevant data of each PPE; and
4. Direct the GSO and Accounting Office to conduct reconciliation of their
records regularly/periodically so that discrepancies/unreconciled items,
if any, are immediately established and adjusted, in compliance with
the above cited circular.

We recommended the following:
a. For machineries totaling ₱2,846,649.08 already covered by MOA,
management shall instruct the Municipal Agriculturist to
formalize the transfer of machineries thru the execution of Deed of
Donations between the LGU and the farmers’ association;
b. For machineries with total NBV of ₱989,233.99 already distributed
but not yet covered by any legal documents, require the Municipal
Agriculturist to expedite the preparation and execution of the
transfer documents for the said PPE; and
c. For the hand tractors costing ₱906,990.00, direct the Inventory
Committee and Municipal Agriculturist to make all the necessary
efforts to verify the existence, condition and ownership of the
machineries.
Henceforth, the Municipal Agriculturist shall forward the copies of all
legal documents supporting the transfer of ownership of the machineries to the
Municipal Accountant for proper derecognition and the preparation of necessary
adjusting entries.
We also recommended that the LCE direct the Inventory Committee to
properly conduct physical count to verify the existence, condition and ownership
of all PPE. Furthermore, the committee shall prepare and submit the RPCPPE,
reflecting the actual results of the physical count and reconcile it with accounting
records to identify any discrepancies.

We recommended that the Management:
a. Direct the concerned LGU officials to fully settle their advances to
comply with Section 89 of PD 1445 and the pertinent provisions of COA
Circular 97-002;
b. Require the Municipal Accountant to send notices 10 days before
expiration of the 30-day period to concerned personnel to settle their
accounts or face possible administrative and legal sanctions for non-
compliance despite notice; and
c. Strictly enforce the laws, rules and regulations on the grant, utilization
and liquidation of cash advances.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive as the Special
Disbursing Officer and the Municipal Accountant strictly observe the rules and
regulations in the grant and liquidation of CF as prescribed under pertinent
provisions of COA-DBM-DILG-GCG-DND Joint Circular No. 2015-1 dated
January 8, 2015.

We recommended that the Inventory Committee conduct physical count
of inventory for all supplies and materials and prepare the RPCI for every
semester of a given year in accordance with NGAS Manual and submit the same
to the Audit Team and also to Accounting Office for reconciliation of records.

We recommended that Management consider designating a stockroom or
storage space for supplies and materials to ensure proper storage and inventory
management.
We also recommended that the GSO designate and Accounting Office to
strictly adhere to Section 114, 119 and 120 of the NGAS Manual for LGUs,
Volume I on the preparation and maintenance of stock cards and SLC with stock
number on each type of supplies as prescribed in the above manual.
Further, the Accounting Office shall adopt the perpetual inventory method
as required in Section 114 of the NGAS Manual for LGUS, Volume I.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant
and Municipal Treasurer to:
a. Closely coordinate in reconciling the unremitted taxes under the 20%
Development Fund and Trust Fund totaling ₱53,606.62 and ensure its
timely settlement;
b. Conduct further reconciliation of the negative balance of the Due to
BIR account under the General Fund and make the necessary
adjusting entries to reflect the correct balance of the account; and
c. Ensure adherence to BIR regulations and deadlines to avoid penalties,
surcharges, and interest charges on delayed or incomplete
remittances.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
a. Instruct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to
conduct reconciliation of the previous years’ balances of the Due to GSIS
and Pag-ibig Accounts and immediately remit all premium contributions
and loan amortizations to the aforementioned agencies;
b. Direct the Municipal Accountant to ensure that any adjustments made
are supported by proper reconciliation and approval to maintain the
integrity of financial records; and
c. Require the Municipal Treasurer to ensure that all withheld employee
contributions and loan amortizations, along with the corresponding
government shares, are remitted within the prescribed period to avoid
penalties and interest charges.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal
Accountant to continuously conduct review and analysis of the long outstanding
Guaranty/Security Deposits Payables and coordinate with concerned personnel
for proper disposition and, if warranted, release immediately the retention money
to the concerned suppliers in accordance with Section 62.1 of the Revised IRR of
RA No. 9184. However, if suppliers could not be located anymore, the retained
amounts are deemed as invalid claims, hence, should be reverted back to un-
appropriated surplus.

We recommended that Management:
a. Submit a written justification as to why the capital gains and estate
taxes amounting to ₱104,778.35 in relation to the acquisition of lot
from private individuals were paid by the Municipality, despite non-
stipulation of such provision in the contract of sale;
b. Require the Municipal Planning Development Coordinator, the
Municipal Engineer and other concerned LGU officials to closely
monitor the implementation of priority development projects and
programs under the 20% DF and immediately report and address
issues that caused the delay of its completion; and
c. Observe strictly the timelines of implementation of PPAs as indicated
in the Annual Investment Plan to guarantee prompt completion
thereof for the benefit of the general public to comply with Section 4
of JMC No. 2020-1 of the DBM, the DILG and the DOF.

We commended the Management for substantial implementation of
LDRRM projects in compliance with Rule 1, Sections 3 (e) and Rule 4, Section
4(1) of the Implementing Rules and Regulations of RA 10121.
Likewise, we recommended that Management should strive for total
implementation of all projects embodied in the 5% LDRRM Fund.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct:
1. HR Officer to:
a. Formulate policies and procedures in the recruitment and
engagement of JO personnel to strengthen the internal control
pertaining thereto; and
b. Conduct personnel review, assessment and evaluation of the
existing manpower complement of the LGU to determine the
necessity and propriety of hiring JO personnel.
2. Municipal Accountant to:
a. Ensure that payments for wages of JO personnel are supported by
adequate documentation including duly approved accomplishment
reports and Daily Time Records.

We recommended the Management to:
a. Require the BAC and its Technical Working Group to submit written
justifications on the above-mentioned observations;
b. Instruct the BAC-TWG to strengthen its post-qualification process by
conducting comprehensive evaluation of winning bidder’s legal and
technical capability.
c. Submit written justification why there was no price reduction despite the
evident downgrade in the technical specifications of the delivered
motorboats;
d. Direct the Municipal Accountant to be circumspect in the processing of
claims so that liquidated damages could be imposed to suppliers; and
e. Ensure strict adherence to the specifications or minimum requirements
outlined in the contract for all future procurement activities.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Engineer to:
1. Submit a written justification regarding the lack of sufficient
documentations supporting the request and approval of the
contract extension;
2. Ascertain that the computation of mobilization costs for all
infrastructure projects does not exceed 1% of EDC of civil work
items, in accordance with DPWH Department DO No. 197 series
of 2016; and
3. Ensure that all changes/revisions made to contracts for the
implementation of infrastructure projects are adequately
supported by required documentations as prescribed by the
aforementioned rules and regulations.

We recommended that Management:
a. Direct the Municipal Engineer to include the Construction Safety and
Health Program in the computation of construction costs of all projects
to be implemented by the Municipality;
b. Instruct the BAC to ensure that contractors submit DOLE-approved
CSHP for all infrastructure projects as an integral part of the contract
as required by Item 37.2.3 (g) of the RIRR of RA 9184; and
c. Initiate the reporting of contractors found to be violating the safety
rules and regulations to the Philippine Contractors Accreditation
Board (PCAB) pursuant to Section 29 of the DOLE DO No. 198, series
of 2018.

We recommended the following:
1. For the utilization of the sports equipment, management to direct the
Municipal Tourism Operations Officer to coordinate with the
Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator in developing a
comprehensive set of plans detailing the proposed activities and
programs in the Bato River Park consistent with the LGU’s overall
program; and
2. For the utilization of the solid waste equipment, management to require
the MENRO-Designate to coordinate with concerned officials to fast-
track the energization of the dumpsite to ensure the immediate
utilization of the equipment and fulfill its intended purpose.
We also recommended that Management ensure that government resources
and properties are effectively utilized and properly managed in accordance with
Section 2 of PD No. 1445 to mitigate the risk of obsolescence, which could lead to
additional repair and maintenance expenses and wastage of government funds.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and the concerned municipal
officials:
1. Discontinue the payment of registration fees to various leagues and
associations for the conduct of regular and routinary meetings;
2. Consider proposing to their respective leagues that the conduct of 1-
day meetings may be done through teleconferencing to eliminate the
incurrence of traveling expenses and registration fees, or holding them
at government-operated function halls, session halls and event centers
to minimize costs; and
3. Exercise due prudence in the disbursement of government funds to
avoid incurrence of unnecessary expenditures.

submit to the Appraisal and Disposal Committee to facilitate the appraisal and disposal of unserviceable properties.  Thereafter, we recommended that the Appraisal and Disposal Committee expedite the disposal process in strict compliance with the applicable guidelines.


Lastly, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor provide a designated storage area for all unserviceable items pending disposal. This space should provide adequate protection from rain and other environmental elements that may accelerate the deterioration of the assets while awaiting proper disposal.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant comply with section 59 of NGAS in the treatment of stale checks. 




We also recommended the Municipal Accountant to reconcile the accounting records with the accounting reports and make adjusting entries, if necessary.



Moreover, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor, through the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant, investigate the additional bank account reflected in the Bank Confirmation Reply from the Development Bank of the Philippines (DBP) – Maasin City Branch and provide a copy of the results of the investigation to the Audit Team.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the concerned official who caused the delay of remittance which resulted to the payment of interest/ surcharge to refund the amount of ₱5,101.93.  The refund should be issued with Official Receipt (AF 51) and consequently, deposited to the account of the LGU.

Moreover, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant, together with the Disbursing Officer, trace back their records and verify the accuracy of the negative beginning balance of ₱38,170.28 and issue a communication letter to PhilHealth regarding the unsettled premium listed in Table 6.

Lastly, we recommended that the SDO, under the supervision and in close coordination with the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer, ensure the prompt and full remittance of all amounts withheld from employees' salaries, including the LGU's corresponding employer share. This should be done in strict compliance with the deadlines set by PhilHealth to prevent penalties and protect employees' entitlement to benefits

We recommended that the Human Resource Management Officer (HRMO) promptly coordinate with the Disbursing Officer, who is also the payroll preparer, to update personnel’s employment status and/or salary adjustments.

Further, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the Pag-IBIG remittance in charge trace back their records and reconcile the variances listed in Tables 7 and 8.  Verified unremitted premium contributions and loan amortizations must be remitted to Pag-IBIG immediately, while for those remittances made even without equivalent deduction from employees, if any, the corresponding personnel must refund the amount paid from LGU fund.

Moreover, we recommended that the personnel responsible for Pag-IBIG remittance, under the supervision of and in coordination with the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer, ensure the full and timely remittance of the amounts withheld from employees and the recorded employer share of LGU Bato, in accordance with the deadline set by Pag-IBIG.

We recommended that the Disbursing Officer and the Human Resource and Management Officer (HRMO), who are also the Municipality’s AAOs:

a. Regularly reconcile records with GSIS and promptly update employees on any changes to their employment status and salary adjustments. 

b. Ensure timely updates in the GSIS system to maintain accurate records.


c. Monitor the approval of employees' loan applications and immediately inform payroll preparer to ensure timely deductions from payroll.

Furthermore, we recommended that the Disbursing Officer responsible for payroll preparation ensure that all premium contributions and loan amortizations are deducted from employees' salaries in the month they are due.

Also, the Remittance in Charge should remit the amount withheld with the employer’s contribution to GSIS, within the prescribed deadline.

Further, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the GSIS remittance in charge trace back their records and reconcile the variances listed in Tables 9 and 10 and the negative balances listed in Table 11.  Verified unremitted premium contributions and loan amortizations must be remitted to GSIS immediately, while for those remittances made even without equivalent deduction from employees, if any, the corresponding personnel must refund the amount paid from LGU fund.  In addition, prepare journal entries to correct the misstatements discussed in paragraph 5.3.8.

Finally, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the GSIS remittance in-charge conduct monthly reconciliations of employee premium contributions and loan amortizations by comparing GSIS billings with actual payroll deductions and remitted amounts. This practice ensures billing accuracy, facilitates timely remittances to GSIS, and guarantees correct salary deductions.

We recommended that the BAC properly prepare all the necessary documents required in every step of the procurement process.




We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure that all the necessary documentary requirements for the payment of development projects are complete and submitted before affixing her signature in the Disbursement Voucher and certifying that the supporting documents attached are complied.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Environment and Natural Resources Officer ensure the compliance of the necessary documents required by COA Circular No. 2012-001 for the acquisition of land and submit the lacking documents enumerated in Paragraph 1.2 to the Audit Team.

LACKING DOCS FOR SUBMISSION:
1. Certification form MPDO that the claim is note within the original ROW of a converted local road
2. Certification from MO that there is no previous pending claim and previous payment on subject property.
3. ECC c/o menro

We recommended that the MDRRMO, in coordination with the Municipal Engineer, ensure the proper geotagging of all infrastructure projects funded under the MDRRM Fund, in strict compliance with the provisions of Department of Public Works and Highways (DPWH) Department Order No. 79, series of 2017, to promote transparency and accurate project monitoring.

Also, we recommended that the Local Legislative Body of the Municipality of Bato through the MDRRMO furnish copy of the Sanggunian Resolution on the declaration of a State of Calamity to the Regional DRRM Council and the National DRRM Council. 

Further, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor and the MDRRMO charge GIF of the GSIS insurance premium payment to the General Fund.

Lastly, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor and the MDRRMO refrain from utilizing the MDRRM Fund to cover expenses for non-disaster related expenditures.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor issue an Executive Order for the creation of GAD Monitoring and Evaluation Team.




Also, we recommended that the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator, in coordination with the GFPS and the DILG Operations Field Officer assigned to the LGU, systematically collect and consolidate updated data on gender statistics, including age and sex-disaggregated data. This will facilitate the creation and maintenance of a comprehensive GAD Database to support evidence-based planning, implementation, and monitoring of gender-responsive programs and activities.

Further, we recommended that the GFPS ensure the timely preparation of the Annual Status Report on Institutional Mechanisms for Gender Mainstreaming and submit it to the DILG Regional Office within the prescribed deadline.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, in coordination with the HRMO, undertake the following measures:

a. Develop and issue written policy guidelines outlining the standard procedures for hiring Job Order (JO) personnel to ensure an objective and impartial selection process, eliminating any form of bias or favoritism.

b. Ensure that the required documents to be submitted by applicants should be clearly specified to maintain consistency and transparency.

c. Ensure that the guidelines should include a detailed job description in the Contract of Service to assure that JO personnel understand their roles and responsibilities.

d. Establish other necessary internal control measures that would outline to protect public resources, ensuring the efficient and effective use of funds.

The development and implementation of these guidelines will streamline the hiring process, promoting transparency and accountability in the selection of Job Order personnel.

We also recommended that once the policies and/or manual on hiring of job order is prepared, a copy thereof be submitted to the audit team.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor take proactive measures to ensure that all insurable LGU properties are covered under the General Insurance Fund (GIF) of the Government Service Insurance System (GSIS), in compliance with R.A. No. 656 and the guidelines outlined in COA Circular 2018-002 to mitigate the risk of losses resulting from natural and man-made calamities.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor reconsider the granting of honoraria to the PNP personnel who do not hold the position of Police Station Commander, as this is the only role eligible for such payments under the afore-cited guidelines.

We recommended that the Disbursing Officer, in coordination with the HRMO and Accountant, review the net take-home pay of employees applying for loans before processing any documents required for loan approval via salary deductions. If the net take-home pay is found to be below the minimum threshold specified in the GAA, the loan application should be automatically rejected.


Furthermore, we recommended that the Disbursing Officer discontinue the practice of deducting payments for contributions and/or obligations from employees' salaries in a way that causes their monthly net take-home pay to fall below ₱5,000.00. This should be done in accordance with the order of preference specified in Section 56 of the General Provisions of the General Appropriations Act for Fiscal Year 2024, as well as in the GAA for subsequent years.

We recommended that the that the Municipal Mayor, through the Human Resource and Management Officer and Municipal Treasurer adopt electronic payroll system through the Automated Teller Machine (ATM) of Government Banks for the payment of salaries, honoraria, allowances, bonuses, and wages of regular employees, elected officials, and job order workers.

We recommended that the Chairman of the Inventory Committee submit written justification for the above-mentioned deficiencies.
We also recommended that the management avail the one-time cleansing of PPE in accordance with COA Circular 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020 as soon as possible, in order to settle the long outstanding discrepancy in the PPE account and to take immediate appropriate action on disposal of unserviceable PPEs to avoid further deterioration and loss of their value.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the official concerned to submit to the audit team the as-built plans prepared by appropriate person/party for the completed infrastructure projects as required in the aforementioned guidelines.  This recommendation is without prejudice to the issuance of a Notice of Suspension and/or Disallowance if warranted.
Moving forward, we recommended that payment of honoraria should be supported with the necessary supporting documents as provided in COA Circular 2023-004 (This circular is still suspended)

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct all personnel concerned to stop the withdrawal of fuel, oil and lubricants without perfected contracts in accordance with RA 9184, and to stop advance payment unless allowed by the Philippine Law.
Henceforth, we recommended that procurement of fuel, oil and lubricants be meticulously and judiciously planned by the Municipality to avoid unlawful expenses.
We also recommended that the BAC must check thoroughly all the Legal, Technical and Financial Documents of all Bidders before the award and strictly adhere to the provisions of RA 9184.
We recommended that the Management to henceforth observe the above-mentioned guidelines in the procurement of all nonmetallic minerals and quarry resources.
We recommended that the Management submit a written justification why was the reclamation project was pursued even without the necessary permits and clearances.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor mandate each LGU Officials responsible in the accounting and reporting of the local road network of the LGU to strictly follow the procedures outlined in COA Circular No. 2015-008 and monitor their compliance thereof.
We also recommended that:

The Municipal Engineer provide the Municipal Accountant and the Property Officer with the complete description and cost segregation of road components of road projects. 
The Municipal Accountant maintain the Local Road Network Ledger Cards with complete data/description of each component of the local road network and compute the depreciation per component of the road network.
The Property Officer prepare and maintain complete Local Road Network Property Cards with the necessary data and prepare the Report on the Local Road Network.
The Inventory Team to prepare and submit the Report on the Physical Count of the Road Network System in accordance with the format provided under COA Circular 2015-008

We recommended for the Inventory Committee Chairperson/Property Custodian and Municipal Accountant to collaborate with the Municipal Assessor and Municipal Engineer in reconciling their records and verifying the LGU's fixed assets. This is to address the items that were not included in the Report of the Physical Count and ensure their existence. Adjustments and corrections should be implemented in the books and property records to ensure proper account management. 

Furthermore, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor, Municipal Accountant and Property Custodian evaluate the list of non-existing/missing PPEs prepared by the Inventory Team and start the disposition procedures for non-existing/missing PPEs.

We recommended that the Inventory Committee update the currently applied property number to the prescribed uniform identification system for PPE, prepare new property tags for all PPEs which shall contain the information enumerated in COA Circular 2020-006 Section 5.7, and attach the same to the PPEs.  

Further, we recommended that that a member of the Inventory Committee should sign the property stickers during the annual inventory of PPEs and validation process. This will help ensure accountability and proper documentation of the inventory proceedings.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Management:

stop charging from LGU funds the power and water consumption expenses incurred by the Municipal Fire Station;

direct the GSO in coordination with the head of the BFP-Bato to request the transfer of name of electric subscriber from the Municipal Treasurer’s Office to BFP if warranted or

direct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to bill the Municipal Fire Station for their monthly power and water consumption and account the same as receivable.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the BAC to conduct careful evaluation of all related procurement transactions and recommend the more appropriate mode of procurement. The BAC shall be guided by the definition of “regular and ordinary office supplies” in resorting to shopping as stipulated in Sec. 52.2 of the Revised IRR of RA 9184.

We recommended that Management:
direct the Municipal Accountant to reclassify the unsupported and undocumented amount of ₱51,564.51 from the Cash-Local Treasury   account to the Other Receivables account; and
require the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to reconcile entries in the Cashbook and General Ledger/Subsidiary Ledger at least on a quarterly basis and trace the variance of ₱129,713.23 and any significant findings should be resolved accordingly.

We recommended that Management require all Accountable Officers to settle their respective cash advances at the end of the year to reflect the correct balances of the accounts affected in the books of accounts of the Agency.  Henceforth, avoid granting subsequent cash advances to Accountable Officers with outstanding cash advances and withhold the salaries and/or any amount due to those Accountable Officers who were unable to comply with the pertinent rules and regulations on cash advances.  Adhere strictly to the guidelines in the granting, utilization, and settlement of cash advances as enunciated in COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997.
We further recommended the immediate liquidation of the outstanding cash advances amounting to ₱ 606,236.45 as of December 31, 2023, and submit the liquidation report or proof of refund/s, if there is any, to the Auditor for post-audit.

We recommended that Management avail the one-time cleansing of the dormant accounts covered in COA Circular 2023-008 to properly dispose of the dormant accounts being maintained in the books of the Agency.
We further recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare and submit the quarterly SDA to the Audit Team and ensure that those below ten years are properly documented and appropriately acted upon to avoid being dormant.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant trace and analyze the transactions, and reconcile the difference of ₱9,872,164.11 in the reciprocal accounts Due to/Due from Other Funds and prepare the necessary adjustments to reflect the correct balances of the accounts Due from Other Funds and Due to Other Funds as of December 31, 2023.

We recommended that Management:
Require the Municipal Accountant to cause the reversion to the unappropriated surplus of the identified long outstanding payables where the obligation to pay has already been prescribed; and
Coordinate with the concerned personnel about the long outstanding guaranty/security deposits payable and facilitate the prompt release of retention monies to prevent accumulation of balances of the said account.

We recommended that the Management:
Observe adequate planning of DRRM PPAs, including but not limited to the specific identification of the type, volume, and estimated cost of items to be purchased or works to be accomplished under each PPA, inclusion or consolidation thereof to the APP, and determination of the timing and proper mode of procurement, to facilitate the prompt and economic implementation of such PPAs;
Ascertain that the LDRRMF is utilized optimally through the determination and implementation of disaster- responsive PPAs to ensure the attainment of the objectives of RA 10121; and
Include the unexpended DRRMF for the past five years in its annual LDRRMFIP to fund the continuation of partially implemented and unimplemented DRRM PPAs, and other identified PPAs by the LGU under the four thematic areas of DRRM.

We recommended that management:
Cease and desist from using the Calamity Fund earmarked for the delivery of disaster risk reduction programs and activities for unrelated and inappropriate disbursements to avoid adverse actions in audit; and
direct all Municipal officials to judiciously plan, allocate, and implement PPAs under the five percent LDRRMF solely and strictly for disaster prevention and mitigation, preparedness, response, rehabilitation, and recovery purposes to ensure maximum utilization of funds in compliance with existing law, rules, and regulations.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer to strictly comply with the requirements of the above regulations for the proper monitoring of the form accountabilities of each accountable officer and to ensure the prompt and timely submission of said reports.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to:
Deduct the ten percent retention money from each progress payment for infrastructure projects up to 50 percent completion of the works, and impose additional warranty on delayed and unsatisfactory works beyond 50 percent; and
Release the retention money only after final acceptance of the project or upon request of the contractor to substitute the same with an irrevocable standby letter of credit or surety bond callable on demand or other equally acceptable documents allowed by the above-cited RIRR.

We recommended that Management prioritize the implementation and completion, preferably within the budget year, of all priority development projects/programs funded out of the 20 percent EDF to attain the desired socio-economic development and environmental outcomes of the LGU.
We recommended the Management ensure timely and accurate remittance of PhilHealth contributions to prevent the recurrence of unremitted balances.

We recommended that the Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to cause the immediate remittance to Pag-IBIG or make the necessary adjustment in the books of accounts as the case may be.
Henceforth, we recommended that the Management require the Municipal Accountant to ensure that all Pag-IBIG contributions and/or loan payments withheld from officials and employees are remitted on time.

We recommended that Management install proper tarpaulin signboards at project sites for the project duration to notify the public of the essential features of GPPAs that are being implemented by the LGU in a given year/s following the provisions of COA Circular No. 2013-004 to promote transparency and accountability.

We recommended that Management stop the practice of accommodating the payment for personal loans of LGU officials and employees with private financial institutions and associations which run afoul of the above-cited law, rules and regulations.
Officials and employees who availed loans from private banks should settle their obligations directly with the banks since these transactions are not official in nature.
We also recommended that utilization of scarce government resources should be directed to more publicly beneficial projects/programs/activities that provide tangible advantage, productivity, or benefit to the general populace rather than mere transitory benefit to a select few.

We recommended that Management prioritize the construction of a garage/motor pool to shelter its vehicles and heavy equipment and protect them against the elements of nature as well as to safeguard the same against damage due to vandalism, misuse/unauthorized use, and possible theft.
Further, we recommended that the logo of the Municipality and the markings “FOR OFFICIAL USE ONLY” be placed on the motor vehicles of the LGU in accordance with the specifications cited in COA Circular No. 75-6.

We recommended the following:
a. 	The Local Chief Executive (LCE) to direct the Inventory Committee to complete the conduct of physical inventory and render report thereon as required under Section 124, Chapter 7, Volume I of the NGAS Manual.  The progress of the inventory taking should be monitored by the LCE and issue warning to the Committee members that failure to perform their duties may be a valid ground for filing of administrative charges for gross neglect of duty.
b. 	Thereafter, the Inventory Committee, the General Services Office (GSO) and the Municipal Accounting Office reconcile all the inventory reports with the recorded PPE in the books of accounts; and
c. 	Require the previous Chairman of the Inventory Committee to explain their failure to conduct inventory and render report thereon.

We recommended that the LCE as the Special Disbursing Officer and the Municipal Accountant to strictly observe the rules and regulations in the grant and release of CF of the municipality prescribed under pertinent provisions of COA-DBM-DILG-GCG-DND Joint Circular No. 2015-1 dated January 8, 2015.

We recommended that the LCE direct:
The concerned employees to fully settle their advances to comply with the Section 89 of PD 1445 and the pertinent provisions of COA Circular 97-002; and
The Municipal Accountant to refrain from processing advances for travel unless previous cash advances are fully settled.

We recommended the following:
For the operation of the Barangay Health Station, the LCE to instruct the Municipal Health Officer to coordinate with the Department of Health regarding the provision of necessary materials and equipment to fully operate the facility.
Also, the LCE direct the Municipal Engineering Office to ensure that billboards installed at project sites contain the required information in accordance with COA Circular No. 2013-004.
For the utilization of the Water Sports Equipment, the LCE instruct the Municipal Tourism Operations Officer to finalize the operations manual for the usage, maintenance and disposal of the equipment.
Also, direct the Municipal Tourism Operations Officer to coordinate with the Municipal Planning and Development Officer in developing a comprehensive set of plans detailing the proposed activities and programs in the Bato River Park consistent with the LGU’s overall program.
For the distribution of the Agricultural Machineries, the LCE instruct the Municipal Agriculturist to distribute the acquired machineries and specify in the Memorandum of Agreement between the LGU and the beneficiary organizations the status of ownership of the equipment. If ownership is retained by the LGU or if transferred to the beneficiary, the MOA should stipulate that this could not be transferred or sold and ensure that regular maintenance be made for the equipment to be in good running condition.
We also recommended that Management through the Local Development Council carefully and judiciously plan the developmental projects to be charged to the 20% DF to ensure optimization of resources and for beneficiaries to attain maximum benefit pursuant to Section 2 of PD 1445 and Section 305g of RA 1760.

We recommended that Management:
Strengthen the monitoring scheme for infrastructure projects to minimize if not avoid delays, contract termination and lapse of fund validity;
Impose the appropriate sanctions/penalties against the contractor in accordance with Item 6, Appendix 17 of the Updated 2016 Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations or Republic Act No. 9184 (Updated as of July 03, 2023; and
Prospectively, ensure that fund transfers from LGSF Programs are utilized within its validity period to effectively achieve its intended purpose and ensure the timely delivery of project benefits.

We recommended that Management:
Submit documents/proof to show that the said training/workshop could only be conducted outside the province e.g. unavailability of speakers, internet instability; and
Refrain from holding trainings/workshops outside the province when this could be conducted within to encourage prudence in the disbursement of government funds to avoid incurrence of extravagant expenditures.

We recommended that the LCE:
Require the BAC to explain the adoption of shopping as the mode of procurement for the transactions mentioned; and
Direct the BAC to conduct careful evaluation of all procurement transactions and recommend the appropriate mode of procurement to avoid possible suspensions or disallowances of the payments made.

We recommended that the LCE require the Municipal Engineer to prepare the documents required in Annex A of COA Circular 2009-001, the supporting papers to validate project accomplishments for every infrastructure project implemented by administration pursuant to Item 3.1.3, Appendix 1 of the RIRR of RA 9184. Afterwards, submit the same to the Audit Team for review, evaluation and validation.

We recommended that the LCE:
Instruct the GSO to submit to the Municipal Accounting Office copies of the disbursement vouchers and its supporting documents pertinent to the transactions totaling ₱966,200.88 for recording of the appropriate inventory and payables in the books of accounts;
Direct the Municipal Budget Officer to submit to the Municipal Accountant a list of not yet due and demandable obligations as of year-end for disclosure in the Notes to Financial Statements; and
Require the Municipal Accountant to ensure that all goods/services delivered to the Municipality which remained unpaid at the end of the year are properly accounted in the books of the Municipality.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Engineer to furnish the Municipal Accountant with the list of completed projects at year-end so that the latter could reclassify the CIP account to the appropriate PPE accounts and achieve fair presentation of the asset accounts in the financial statements.

We recommended that Management:
Provide a stock room for proper storage of items of inventories or if no storage space is identified, require the end-user units to maintain a record of deliveries and issuances and submit the same to the GSO through the weekly preparation of SSMIs supported by the RIS. The SSMIs will then be consolidated by the GSO and submitted to the MA for recording of the actual issuances in the books of accounts and will help to determine the inventory balances as of a given date; and
Require the Inventory Committee to conduct semestral physical inventory of supplies and materials and submit the Physical Inventory Report to the Auditor’s Office as required in Section 124, Volume 1 of the MNGAs.

We recommended that the LCE:
Require the concerned personnel to secure certification from the LCE on the necessity of the excess claim for accommodation (Annex F), justify the travel made (Annex G) and refund the unnecessary expenses claimed (Annex H);
Direct the Municipal Accountant to ensure that necessary documents are complete before requests for reimbursement or liquidations for travels are processed; and
Ensure that all travels of officials and employees charged to the funds of the LGU are essential to the effective performance of the official or employee’s functions and meets the criteria stated in Section 3 of EO No. 77.

We recommended that the LCE:
Direct the Municipal Accountant to furnish the MTO with the schedule of funds for transfer to the barangays at the end of each quarter as basis for preparation of checks; and
Instruct the Municipal Treasurer to facilitate the preparation of checks releasing the 25 % RPT shares of the barangays within 5 days after the end of each quarter to comply with Item 4, Section 271 of Republic Act No. 7160.

1.7. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Treasurer
to:
a) Stop the practice of encashing private checks using local collections;
b) Deposit all collections intact and daily in accordance with the above-
mentioned regulation;
c) Instruct all collecting officers to remit all their collections daily for
deposit and to prepare the Report of Collections and Deposits on daily
basis;
d) Make sure that collecting officers are not issued additional accountable
forms (AF) unless collections from previously issued AF are remitted
intact;
e) Stop designating as collectors, those who habitually disregard the
mentioned rules, laws and regulations; and
f) Perform diligently his duties and responsibilities as the Municipal
Treasurer.

2.7. We recommended that the Chairman of the Inventory Committee submit
written justification for the above-mentioned deficiencies.

2.8. We also recommended that the management avail the one-time cleansing of
PPE in accordance with COA Circular 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020 as
soon as possible, in order to settle the long outstanding discrepancy in the PPE
account and to take immediate appropriate action on disposal of unserviceable
PPEs to avoid further deterioration and loss of their value.

3.9. We recommended that Management should strictly observe the guidelines on
the use of Confidential Fund as set forth in COA-DBM-DILG-GCG-DND
Joint Circular No. 2015-01 dated January 8, 2015. This recommendation is
without prejudice to the issuance of a Notice of Suspension and/or
Disallowance if warranted.

3.10. During the exit conference conducted on March 6, 2024, the Management has well-
noted the audit recommendations.

4.8. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require all the officers and
employees involve to submit written justification for the above-mentioned
noncompliance and henceforth, be mindful of Presidential Decree 1445 and
comply with all the conditions set in the MOA.

5.6. We recommended that management continue to strictly adhere with the
aforementioned provisions on the use of the 20% Development Fund.

6.7. We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the official concerned
to submit to the audit team the as-built plans prepared by appropriate
person/party for the completed infrastructure projects as required in the
aforementioned guidelines. This recommendation is without prejudice to the
issuance of a Notice of Suspension and/or Disallowance if warranted.

7.8. We recommended that the Municipality continue to strictly observe the
relevant regulations in the handling and recording of LDRRM Fund.

8.8. We recommended that the LDRRM Officer:
a) submit the LDRRMFIP/LDRRMFP for CY 2023, and
b) submit revised AIP as approved by the Local Sanggunian and Local
Chief Executive upon recommendation of the LDRRMC, in accordance
with the approved LDRRMP to support for the use of unexpended
LDRRMF, if PPAs are not included in the CY 2023 LDRRMFIP.

9.8. We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the MDRRM Officer
submit to the Audit Team the lacking documents listed in paragraphs 1.5 and
1.6.

9.9. Moving forward, we recommended that payment of honoraria should be
supported with the necessary supporting documents as provided in COA
Circular 2023-004.

10.10. We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer, Municipal Accountant,
Municipal Engineer and the Chairman of the Bids and Awards Committee
submit a written explanation/justification for the deficiencies mentioned
above.

10.11. We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct all personnel
concerned to stop the withdrawal of fuel, oil and lubricants without perfected
contracts in accordance with RA 9184, and to stop advance payment unless
allowed by the Philippine Law.

10.12. Henceforth, we recommended that procurement of fuel, oil and lubricants be
meticulously and judiciously planned by the Municipality to avoid unlawful
expenses.

10.13. This audit observation is without prejudice to the issuance of a Notice of
Suspension/Disallowance, if warranted.

11.6. We recommended that the Municipal Budget Officer submit a written
justification for deviating from the above-mentioned guidelines, as it is his
responsibility to advise the Executive and Legislative bodies of the
Municipality on proper procedures, which should have been adhered to in this
case.

11.7. We also recommended that management determine its intention on these
vacant positions considering that these are not yet funded for CY 2024 Annual
Budget.

12.6. We recommended that the Municipal Budget Officer submit a written
justification on the incurrence of excess PS limitation.

12.7. We recommended that the Municipal Budget Officer and the Local Finance
Committee observe the PS Limitation prescribed under the aforementioned
guidelines in the preparation of Annual and Supplemental Budgets.

13.8. We recommended that the BAC submit written justification why these
procurements were awarded to supplier with inconsistent line of business.

13.9. We also recommended that the BAC must check thoroughly all the Legal,
Technical and Financial Documents of all Bidders before the award and
strictly adhere to the provisions of RA 9184.

13.10. Finally, we recommended that the BAC must disqualify any bidder who do
not meet the minimum eligibility requirements to avoid suspension in audit.

14.9. We recommended that the Management to henceforth observe the above-
mentioned guidelines in the procurement of all nonmetallic minerals and
quarry resources.

15.8. We recommended that the Management submit a written justification why
was the reclamation project was pursued even without the necessary permits
and clearances.

16.5. We recommended that the Local Chief Executive or his duly authorized
official to:
a) submit a written justification why Arrastre was acting as third-party
service operator in behalf of the Municipality without the necessary
MOA, accreditation and permit;
b) stop allowing Arrastre to act as third-party service operator until there
is MOA between the LGU and the former with concurrence of the
Legislative Branch;
c) should stop allowing Arrastre to collect port tariff;
d) authorize only bonded collecting officers to collect port tariffs and
require the immediate issuance of Official Receipt of the LGU upon
collection; and
e) strictly observe the provisions stated in PPA Administrative Order No.
08-2020 dated November 23, 2020.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive: 

require the Municipal Planning Development Officer, the Municipal Engineer and other concerned LGU officials to closely monitor the implementation of priority development projects and programs under the 20% DF and immediately report and address issues which delay its completion; and

observe strictly the timelines of implementation of PPAs as indicated in the Annual Investment Plan to guarantee prompt completion thereof for the benefit of the general public to comply with Section 4 of JMC No. 2020-1 of the DBM, the DILG and the DOF.

We recommended that Management:

require the Municipal Health Officer to render a report to the Municipal Accountant on the receipt, utilization and balances of vaccines directly received from outside sources; and

direct the Municipal Accountant, in coordination with the Municipal Health Officer, to determine the full cost of ending inventory of vaccines at year end, if any and henceforth recognize and record future receipts and utilization of the same.

We recommended that the agency head direct the Municipal Health Officer to reconcile the agency records with that of the PHO and make corrections, if necessary.

Also recommended that in future receipts of vaccines or similar supplies from PHO or other sources, the Municipal Health Officer should exert effort to reconcile records with the donor/source for accurate and consistent reporting of information.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Local Finance Committee to immediately post in the LGU’s website and to at least three conspicuous places within the locality the accumulated quarterly Reports on Fund Utilization and Status of PPA Implementation of the LGSF-GFE amounting to ₱4.69 million. 

Henceforth, the Local Chief Executive and Local Finance Committee shall ensure compliance with the posting and reporting requirements relative to funds received by the Municipality to comply with Section 5.0 of LBC No. 146 dated July 7, 2022.

We recommended that Management:

instruct the Municipal Treasurer, in coordination with the Municipal Assessor, to determine the actual amount of tax delinquencies for the current year and from prior years; and

require the Municipal Treasurer to render the necessary report to the Municipal Accountant for proper recognition and recording of tax delinquencies;

direct the Municipal Accountant to set up the RPT and SET Receivables based on the correct report submitted by the Municipal Treasurer.

We recommended that Management:

direct the Human Resource Officer to reevaluate the requirements of the Municipality whether the said personnel shall be employed under a COS agreement or in other categories of employment that will meet the requirements of the existing designation; and

if the said personnel will be hired under a COS agreement, revise the existing contract in accordance with CSC rules and regulations. However, if the personnel will be hired under a contractual/casual/coterminous basis, prepare the necessary documentations and requirements for review and approval of the CSC if the budget may allow.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Local School Board to closely monitor the implementation of all PPAs funded from the SEF to ensure that funds are maximized to support the operation and maintenance of public schools within the Municipality.

We recommended that the Management:

cause the refund of the clothing allowances granted to the newly hired employees totaling ₱18,000.00.

ensure that the grant of similar allowances and other personnel benefits are in accordance with specific rules and regulations to avoid the incurrence of irregular expenditures.

We recommended that the Management:

formulate guidelines and policies to set parameters, conditions and limitations on the grant of financial assistance and cash incentives taking into account the nature, circumstances and purpose of each request. 

instruct the Municipal Accountant to ensure that all grants of assistance and incentives shall be supported with complete and appropriate documentations and;

exercise prudence in the disbursement of funds of the Municipality to ensure that resources are utilized and expended appropriately.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the General Services Officer to submit promptly to COA copies of contracts, purchase orders and notices of deliveries pursuant to COA Circular Nos. 2009-001 and 96-010.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:

direct the BAC Secretariat to accurately prepare a consolidated and approved Annual Procurement Plan in compliance with Sections 7.3.4 and 7.3.5 of RA 9184 to facilitate the procurement of goods and services and of infrastructure projects minimizing delays in delivery of goods and services to intended users and facilitating implementation of projects for the benefit of target beneficiaries; and

require the OIC-Budget Officer to conduct prompt evaluation of the submitted PPMPs and ensure consistency with the Procuring Entity’s budget proposal and compliance with existing budgeting rules.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the BAC to refrain from splitting of government contracts and ensure compliance with the requirements of RA No. 9184 and its Implementing Rules and Regulations to avoid disallowance and possible law suits.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:

instruct the head of requesting offices to indicate only the specifications of the items requested and exclude the brand names in the PRs prepared; and

require the GSO-Designate to evaluate the PRs submitted by the requesting units to check whether brand names are indicated; delete the brand names before consolidation and submission to the BAC and to ensure that only the relevant specifications and performance requirements of goods and services to be purchased are indicated in the RFQs and the Invitation to Bid to comply with Section 18 of RA No. 9184.
